title: Knee Osteoarthritis Home Exercise Program
metaDescription: A printable, evidence-based starter exercise program for knee osteoarthritis with strength, aerobic activity, symptom rules, progression, and safety guidance.
h1: Build a knee that can do more.
summary: A progressive strength and aerobic program is foundational treatment for knee osteoarthritis. This starter plan gives you a practical dose, clear progression, and a next-day symptom rule.

authorityItems.text: Five practical movements for daily function
Strength plus walking or cycling
A six-week checkpoint, then long-term maintenance

proof.span: to look for a functional trend

fitIntro: This starter program is intended for adults who have already been evaluated and told knee osteoarthritis is the likely diagnosis. It is not a self-diagnosis tool, a postoperative protocol, or a substitute for individualized care.
fit: Activity-related knee pain and stiffness, often in adults over 45, with difficulty during walking, stairs, rising from a chair, or prolonged activity.
assessFirst: Recent major injury, a diagnosis that is uncertain, substantial instability, inflammatory arthritis, recent surgery, or pain that is mainly coming from the hip, back, or nerves.
redFlags: A hot red swollen joint, fever, inability to bear weight after trauma, true locking or loss of extension, rapid deformity, marked calf swelling, shortness of breath, or unexplained persistent rest or night pain.
programHeading: Train the tasks your knee needs.
programIntro: Exercise selection has stronger evidence than any single sets-and-repetitions formula. Begin with a dose that keeps movement controlled, then build repetitions, resistance, range, or time gradually.
frequency: Strength 2 to 3 days per week; aerobic activity on most days
equipment: Stable chair, low step, optional band or backpack
goal: Easier walking, stairs, and chair transfers

exercises.name: Sit to stand
exercises.how: Sit near the front of a stable chair. Lean your chest slightly forward, press through both feet, stand tall, then lower with control.
exercises.easier: Use a higher chair or push lightly from the armrests.
exercises.harder: Lower the chair or hold a light backpack at your chest.

exercises.name: Low step-up
exercises.dose: 2 to 3 sets of 6 to 12 repetitions per side
exercises.how: Place one foot on a low step. Keep the knee tracking over the middle toes, rise onto the step, then lower slowly. Use a rail for balance.
exercises.easier: Use a shorter step and more hand support.
exercises.harder: Use a taller step or add light external weight.

exercises.name: Bridge
exercises.how: Lie on your back with knees bent. Tighten your trunk, press through the heels, lift the hips until the body forms a comfortable line, then lower slowly.
exercises.easier: Lift through a smaller range.
exercises.harder: Add a band above the knees or use a staggered foot position.

exercises.name: Seated knee extension
exercises.dose: 2 to 3 sets of 8 to 15 repetitions per side
exercises.how: Sit tall. Slowly straighten one knee, pause with the thigh supported, then lower with control. Keep the movement smooth rather than snapping the knee straight.
exercises.easier: Use a smaller range or fewer repetitions.
exercises.harder: Add a light ankle weight or resistance band.

exercises.name: Walking or cycling base
exercises.how: Choose walking, stationary cycling, swimming, or another rhythmic activity that you can repeat consistently. A shorter session still counts.
exercises.easier: Use two shorter sessions or choose a lower-impact option.
exercises.harder: Add 2 to 5 minutes per week before adding speed or hills.

responseIntro: Mild to moderate discomfort can be acceptable when movement stays controlled and symptoms return to your usual baseline by the next day.
green: Mild discomfort, no new limp, and the knee is back to its usual baseline by the following morning.
yellow: Pain, swelling, or limping is meaningfully worse later that day or remains worse the next morning. Reduce depth, load, repetitions, or aerobic time.
red: Sharp pain, true locking, giving way, a rapidly enlarging swelling, a hot red joint, or inability to bear weight. Stop and seek assessment.

progression.title: Stage 1: establish tolerance
progression.text: Use a comfortable range and the lower end of each repetition range. Prioritize smooth movement and a stable next-day response.

progression.title: Stage 2: build strength
progression.text: Reach the top of the repetition range, then lower the chair, raise the step, add a band or backpack, or slow the lowering phase.

progression.title: Stage 3: build real-world capacity
progression.text: Increase walking or cycling time, add stairs or hills gradually, and practice the specific activities you want to regain.

readyItems: The current dose feels controlled for several sessions.
Symptoms return to baseline by the next morning.
Walking and chair transfers are becoming easier.
You can add one variable without losing movement quality.
evaluation: Schedule a visit if symptoms are worsening, function is not trending better after about six weeks, swelling repeatedly limits exercise, or you need help distinguishing arthritis from another knee problem.

faqs.q: Should I avoid exercise because the knee is worn down?
faqs.a: No. Exercise does not wear out the joint. Progressive strength and aerobic activity are core treatments for knee osteoarthritis and can improve pain, function, and confidence.

faqs.q: Which exercise is best for knee arthritis?
faqs.a: No single mode is universally superior. Strength, aerobic activity, and mobility can all help. The best plan is one you can perform consistently and progress without a sustained symptom flare.

faqs.q: Can I exercise when the knee hurts?
faqs.a: Some discomfort can be acceptable. Use the next-day response: if pain, swelling, or limping remains meaningfully worse the following morning, reduce the dose.
Use six to twelve weeks to build the habit and assess the trend, then continue the useful elements long term. Benefits are maintained through ongoing activity.

sources.label: VA/DoD Clinical Practice Guideline for Non-Surgical Management of Hip and Knee Osteoarthritis, 2026
NICE: Osteoarthritis in Over 16s, Diagnosis and Management
American College of Rheumatology and Arthritis Foundation Guideline for Osteoarthritis
BMJ: Exercise Treatments for Knee and Hip Osteoarthritis Network Meta-analysis, 2025

title: Patellofemoral Pain Home Exercise Program
metaDescription: A printable, evidence-based starter exercise program for patellofemoral knee pain with combined knee and hip strengthening, symptom rules, and progression.
summary: Patellofemoral pain usually responds best to education plus progressive knee and hip strengthening. This plan starts with four movements and a simple next-morning response rule.
authorityTitle: Knee strength. Hip strength. Load control.

authorityItems.text: Combined knee and hip strengthening
Progressive exposure to squatting and stairs
Workload changes that match your irritability

fitIntro: This starter program is intended for adults who have already been evaluated and told patellofemoral pain is the likely diagnosis. It is not a self-diagnosis tool, a postoperative protocol, or a substitute for individualized care.
fit: Pain around or behind the kneecap that is reproduced by squatting, stairs, running, jumping, or prolonged sitting.
assessFirst: A recent traumatic injury, major swelling, recurrent kneecap dislocation, pain focused at the patellar tendon or tibial tubercle, or pain that is not reproduced by loaded knee bending.
redFlags: Traumatic swelling, inability to bear weight, true locking, loss of knee extension, recurrent kneecap dislocation, a hot red joint, fever, or rapidly worsening pain.
programHeading: Share the work across the knee and hip.
programIntro: Begin with more hip-focused work when loaded knee bending is highly irritable. As tolerance improves, gradually restore knee range, repetitions, step height, and resistance.
equipment: Stable chair, low step, optional loop band
checkpoint: Look for a trend by 6 to 8 weeks
goal: More comfortable squatting, stairs, running, and sitting

exercises.name: Sit to stand or shallow squat
exercises.how: Use a chair as a target. Keep the feet grounded, knees tracking over the middle toes, and lower only as far as you can control.
exercises.easier: Use a higher chair or a shallower squat.
exercises.harder: Lower the chair, deepen the squat gradually, or hold light weight.

exercises.name: Low step-down
exercises.dose: 3 sets of 6 to 12 repetitions per side
exercises.how: Stand on a low step with support nearby. Bend the standing knee slowly as the opposite heel reaches toward the floor, then return without the knee collapsing inward.
exercises.easier: Use a shorter step, smaller range, or fingertip support.
exercises.harder: Increase the step height or add a slow three-second lowering phase.

exercises.name: Band lateral walk
exercises.dose: 2 to 3 sets of 8 to 12 steps each direction
exercises.how: Place a loop band above the knees or at the ankles. Keep a small bend in the hips and knees, then step sideways without letting the feet snap together.
exercises.easier: Move the band above the knees or take smaller steps.
exercises.harder: Use a stronger band or place it closer to the ankles.

exercises.name: Bridge
exercises.how: Lie on your back with knees bent. Press through the heels and lift the hips while keeping the pelvis level, then lower with control.
exercises.easier: Use a smaller lift or fewer repetitions.
exercises.harder: Add a loop band or progress toward a staggered bridge.

responseIntro: Keep discomfort mild, approximately 3 out of 10 or less, without limping or changing how you move. Symptoms should not be meaningfully worse the following morning.
green: Mild front-of-knee discomfort, normal movement quality, and no meaningful increase the next morning.
yellow: Pain rises above mild, mechanics change, or stairs and squatting are worse the next day. Reduce knee depth, step height, resistance, or total repetitions.
red: Sharp pain, a kneecap dislocation, true locking, rapid swelling, inability to bear weight, or a hot red joint. Stop and seek assessment.

progression.title: Stage 1: calm the workload
progression.text: Temporarily reduce provocative running, jumping, deep squatting, or hill volume while keeping general activity within tolerance.

progression.title: Stage 2: build repetitions
progression.text: Work toward the upper repetition range with controlled knee alignment before increasing depth, step height, band resistance, or weight.

progression.title: Stage 3: restore the activity
progression.text: Reintroduce running, jumping, stairs, or sport-specific loads in small steps while the next-morning response remains stable.

readyItems: Squats and stairs are becoming less sensitive.
The knee is not meaningfully worse the next morning.
Hip and knee exercises remain controlled at the top repetition range.
You can add one workload variable without a new limp or movement compensation.
evaluation: Schedule a visit if the diagnosis is uncertain, the kneecap repeatedly feels unstable, swelling or locking is present, or there is no improvement trend after six to eight weeks of a consistent program.

faqs.q: Should all knee bending be avoided?
faqs.a: No. The goal is to find a tolerable starting range and reload it progressively. Avoiding all knee bending can reduce capacity and delay return to the activities you value.

faqs.q: Should I strengthen the hip or the knee?
faqs.a: Usually both. Combined knee and hip strengthening is strongly supported. A more hip-focused start can be useful when loaded knee flexion is highly irritable.

faqs.q: Do I need a knee brace or taping?
faqs.a: Exercise and education are primary. Taping or a prefabricated foot orthosis can help selected patients in the short term when matched to the examination, but they do not replace progressive loading.
Return gradually when walking, stairs, and starter strengthening are controlled and the next-morning response is stable. Increase only one running variable at a time.

sources.label: JOSPT Clinical Practice Guideline: Patellofemoral Pain, 2019
American Family Physician: Patellofemoral Pain Guideline Summary
British Journal of Sports Medicine: Best Practice Guide for Patellofemoral Pain, 2024
Winters et al.: Best Practice Guide for Patellofemoral Pain, PubMed Record, 2024

title: Rotator Cuff Related Shoulder Pain Home Exercise Program
metaDescription: A printable, evidence-based starter exercise program for rotator cuff related shoulder pain with range, resistance, symptom rules, and progression.
h1: Restore the shoulder through active loading.
summary: Active motor-control and resistance exercise are first-line care for rotator cuff related shoulder pain. Start with two or three movements when the shoulder is irritable, then progress range and resistance.

authorityItems.text: Comfortable motion before heavier loading
Progressive rotator cuff and shoulder blade strength
Night pain and next-morning response guide the dose

fitIntro: This starter program is intended for adults who have already been evaluated and told rotator cuff related shoulder pain is the likely diagnosis. It is not a self-diagnosis tool, a postoperative protocol, or a substitute for individualized care.
fit: Nontraumatic pain with lifting, reaching, or lying on the shoulder, including many presentations labeled rotator cuff tendinopathy, calcific tendinopathy, or partial-thickness tearing.
assessFirst: A fall or sudden pull followed by major weakness, suspected full-thickness tear, dislocation, fracture, recent surgery, or symptoms mainly driven by the neck or nerves.
redFlags: Trauma followed by immediate weakness or inability to raise the arm, deformity, rapidly progressive weakness, dense numbness, fever or chills, a red swollen shoulder, chest pain, shortness of breath, or systemic illness concerns.
programHeading: Start small, then earn range and resistance.
programIntro: If the shoulder is highly irritable, begin with the wall slide plus one resistance exercise. Add the remaining movements as the next-morning response becomes predictable.
frequency: Motion daily; strength 3 days per week
equipment: Wall, light resistance band, optional light dumbbell
checkpoint: Reassess after 6 weeks; full trial 6 to 12 weeks
goal: More comfortable reaching, lifting, sleep, and sport

exercises.name: Wall slide
exercises.how: Place the forearms on a wall. Gently slide upward while keeping the neck relaxed and ribs controlled, then return slowly through a comfortable range.
exercises.easier: Use a smaller range or assist more with the other arm.
exercises.harder: Reach higher or add gentle pressure into a loop band.

exercises.name: Band external rotation
exercises.how: Keep the elbow at your side with a small towel between the arm and body. Rotate the forearm outward without twisting the trunk, then return slowly.
exercises.easier: Use a lighter band or a shorter range.
exercises.harder: Use a stronger band or slow the return for three seconds.

exercises.name: Band row
exercises.how: Anchor the band in front of you. Pull the elbows back while keeping the shoulders away from the ears, pause, then return with control.
exercises.easier: Stand closer to the anchor or use a lighter band.
exercises.harder: Increase band resistance or use a staggered stance.

exercises.name: Scaption raise
exercises.how: Raise the arm about 30 degrees forward from the side with the thumb pointing up. Stop at a tolerated height, then lower slowly.
exercises.easier: Use no weight and a smaller range.
exercises.harder: Add a light dumbbell or gradually increase the height.

exercises.name: Wall push-up plus
exercises.how: Perform a wall push-up. At the top, gently push the wall away so the shoulder blades move around the ribs, without shrugging.
exercises.easier: Stand closer to the wall.
exercises.harder: Move to a counter, then a higher incline push-up.

responseIntro: Manageable discomfort is acceptable when it settles within a few hours and the shoulder is not worse the next morning.
green: Mild discomfort that settles within a few hours, with stable sleep and daily function the next morning.
yellow: Night pain, reaching, or daily function is worse the next day. Reduce range, resistance, repetitions, or the number of exercises.
red: Sudden loss of strength, inability to raise the arm, deformity, new dense numbness, fever, chest pain, or shortness of breath. Stop and seek assessment.

progression.title: Stage 1: restore comfortable motion
progression.text: Use assisted motion and low resistance. Avoid repeatedly testing the most painful range during the day.

progression.title: Stage 2: build cuff and shoulder blade strength
progression.text: Reach the top repetition range, then progress range first when appropriate, followed by band resistance or light external weight.

progression.title: Stage 3: restore overhead and task-specific load
progression.text: Progress incline pushing, carrying, reaching, throwing, or racquet work in small steps while the next-day response remains stable.

readyItems: Daily reaching is easier and sleep is not worsening.
The exercise dose settles within a few hours.
You can control the full current range without shrugging or trunk compensation.
You can add range or resistance without a next-morning flare.
evaluation: Schedule a visit for traumatic weakness, progressive loss of function, persistent night pain, or substantial pain and disability that have not improved after an appropriate six- to twelve-week active rehabilitation trial.

faqs.q: Do I need an MRI before starting?
faqs.a: Usually not for a typical nontraumatic presentation. Imaging becomes more useful when trauma, major weakness, another diagnosis, or failure to improve would change management.
Not necessarily. Manageable discomfort can be acceptable if it settles within a few hours and the shoulder is not worse the next morning.

faqs.q: Is a supervised program always better than home exercise?
faqs.a: Not always. The GRASP trial found that a supported home program performed similarly to a more intensive supervised progressive program over twelve months. Individual supervision remains valuable when the diagnosis, technique, progression, or adherence is difficult.

faqs.q: When should I consider an injection?
faqs.a: Exercise and education are first-line care. An injection may be discussed selectively when pain prevents rehabilitation or when the diagnosis and expected benefit are clear, but it should not replace progressive loading.

sources.label: JOSPT Clinical Practice Guideline: Rotator Cuff Tendinopathy, 2025
Academy of Orthopaedic Physical Therapy: Full Rotator Cuff Tendinopathy Guideline
GRASP Trial: Progressive Exercise Compared With Best-Practice Advice, 2021

title: Midportion Achilles Tendinopathy Home Exercise Program
metaDescription: A printable, evidence-based starter loading program for midportion Achilles tendinopathy with calf strengthening, symptom monitoring, and progression.
h1: Make the tendon stronger than the demand.
summary: Progressive tendon loading is first-line care for midportion Achilles tendinopathy. This plan uses straight-knee and bent-knee calf work, gradual resistance, and morning stiffness as a load signal.

authorityItems.text: High-tolerance calf loading at least three days per week
Next-morning pain and stiffness guide progression
Running and jumping return after strength foundations

proof.span: straight knee, bent knee, and progression
or more of tendon loading each week

fitIntro: This starter program is intended for adults who have already been evaluated and told midportion Achilles tendinopathy is the likely diagnosis. It is not for postoperative recovery, a suspected rupture, or insertional pain without modification.
fit: Gradual-onset, load-related pain and thickening located about 2 to 6 centimeters above the heel attachment, often with morning stiffness or pain during running and jumping.
assessFirst: Pain directly at the heel attachment, a sudden injury, substantial swelling, inability to perform a heel raise, recent surgery, inflammatory disease, or uncertainty about rupture.
redFlags: A sudden pop, bruising, a palpable gap, inability to push off or perform a heel raise, acute major swelling, calf warmth or swelling, shortness of breath, or acute symptoms with suspected tendon frailty.
programHeading: Use two calf angles and one clear progression.
programIntro: Perform the straight-knee and bent-knee loading exercises on the same day. Use the isometric hold as an entry option on irritable days, not as mandatory extra volume. Keep all work on a flat floor for this midportion starter plan.
frequency: Loading at least 3 days per week
equipment: Wall or counter, chair, optional backpack or dumbbell
goal: Stronger push-off, walking, stairs, running, and jumping

exercises.name: Optional calf-raise isometric hold
exercises.how: Rise onto both forefeet on a flat floor and hold at a comfortable height while using a counter for balance. Keep pressure even across the forefoot.
exercises.easier: Hold lower or shift more weight to the less painful side.
exercises.harder: Shift more weight toward the involved side.

exercises.name: Slow straight-knee heel raise
exercises.how: With knees straight and support nearby, rise for two to three seconds and lower for two to three seconds. Start with both legs.
exercises.easier: Use more hand support or a smaller range.
exercises.harder: Progress to one leg, then add a backpack or dumbbell.

exercises.name: Slow bent-knee heel raise
exercises.how: Keep a small, steady bend in both knees while lifting and lowering the heels slowly. Keep the knees over the middle toes.
exercises.easier: Use a wall sit position with a smaller heel lift.
exercises.harder: Shift toward one leg or add external load.

exercises.name: Single-leg heel raise progression
exercises.how: Rise and lower on one leg with a slow tempo, stable ankle, and similar heel height across repetitions.
exercises.easier: Use the other foot lightly for assistance.
exercises.harder: Add load while preserving height and control.

exercises.name: Later-stage pogo or hop
exercises.frequency: 2 to 3 days per week when ready
exercises.how: Begin with small two-leg springing contacts. Keep the rhythm quiet and controlled. Add this only after walking, stairs, and repeated single-leg heel raises are tolerated.
exercises.easier: Use brisk marching or quick heel raises without leaving the floor.
exercises.harder: Progress toward single-leg or activity-specific hopping.

responseIntro: Some tendon discomfort during loading can be acceptable when mechanics remain controlled and next-morning pain or stiffness does not increase. Complete rest is usually unnecessary.
green: Tolerable discomfort, good heel-raise control, and morning pain or stiffness is unchanged or improving.
yellow: Next-morning pain or stiffness increases, the heel-raise height falls, or running and strengthening together create a cumulative flare. Reduce load or total contacts.
red: A sudden pop, new bruising, a gap, inability to push off, marked calf swelling, or shortness of breath. Stop and seek urgent assessment.

progression.title: Stage 1: establish a tolerable load
progression.text: Use two-leg slow raises and optional isometric holds. Continue general activity within pain tolerance rather than resting completely.

progression.title: Stage 2: increase tendon strength
progression.text: Progress from two legs to one leg, then add external load. Choose a resistance that makes the final repetitions challenging while controlled.

progression.title: Stage 3: restore spring and sport
progression.text: Add fast calf work, pogo contacts, hopping, and running volume only after slow strength and daily activities are stable.

readyItems: Morning pain and stiffness are stable or improving.
Repeated single-leg heel raises have good height and control.
Walking and stairs do not produce a sustained flare.
You can add load without losing the slow tempo.
evaluation: Schedule a visit if the pain is at the heel attachment, the diagnosis is uncertain, symptoms are worsening, a rupture is possible, or a consistent twelve-week loading trial does not improve function.

faqs.q: Should I stretch the tendon over the edge of a step?
faqs.a: Not automatically. This midportion starter plan uses a flat floor. Heel drops below a step can aggravate insertional Achilles pain and should be matched to the diagnosis.

faqs.q: Do I need eccentric heel drops specifically?
faqs.a: No. Eccentric, concentric, heavy slow resistance, and combined loading can all work. The important principle is progressive tendon loading at a challenging, tolerated intensity.
Usually not. Continue activity within pain tolerance when the next-morning response is stable. Reduce running volume or intensity if it repeatedly drives a flare.
Commit to at least a twelve-week loading trial. Recovery commonly takes three to six months, and return to higher-demand sport may take longer.

sources.label: JOSPT Clinical Practice Guideline: Midportion Achilles Tendinopathy, 2024
Beyer et al.: Heavy Slow Resistance Versus Eccentric Training, 2015
Silbernagel et al.: Pain-Monitoring Model and Continued Activity, 2007

title: Gluteal Tendinopathy Home Exercise Program
conditionName: Gluteal tendinopathy and lateral hip pain
metaDescription: A printable, evidence-based starter exercise program for gluteal tendinopathy and lateral hip pain with load management, strengthening, and symptom rules.
kicker: Lateral hip pain · Home exercise plan
h1: Reduce compression, then build hip capacity.
summary: Education plus progressive exercise is first-line care for gluteal tendinopathy. This plan pairs practical position changes with hip and lower-limb strengthening.

authorityItems.text: Reduce sustained compression at the side of the hip
Three progressive hip-strength days each week
Use night pain as a load-management signal

fitIntro: This starter program is intended for adults who have already been evaluated and told gluteal tendinopathy is the likely diagnosis. It is not a self-diagnosis tool, a postoperative protocol, or a substitute for individualized care.
fit: Pain and tenderness around the greater trochanter, often aggravated by side-lying, prolonged walking, stairs, or standing on one leg.
assessFirst: Groin-dominant pain, major hip stiffness, rapidly progressive weakness, a substantial limp, recent trauma, lumbar nerve symptoms, or a diagnosis that remains uncertain.
redFlags: Acute trauma with inability to bear weight, rapidly progressive hip weakness, a marked Trendelenburg gait, fever, deep groin pain with major motion loss, neurologic symptoms, or unexplained systemic or night pain.
programHeading: Change the positions, then train the hip.
programIntro: Temporarily reduce compressive positions such as lying on the painful side, crossing the legs, hanging on one hip, and forceful hip-adduction stretching. Use the isometric on lighter days and the strength movements on nonconsecutive loading days.
frequency: Isometric most days; strength 3 nonconsecutive days per week
equipment: Stable chair, wall, optional loop band or belt
goal: More comfortable sleep, walking, stairs, and single-leg tasks

exercises.name: Hip abduction isometric
exercises.how: Sit with a loop band or belt around the thighs. Press the knees gently outward without moving, hold, then relax. Keep the feet grounded.
exercises.easier: Use less pressure or a shorter hold.
exercises.harder: Increase the hold or band resistance without increasing night pain.

exercises.name: Bridge
exercises.how: Lie on your back with knees bent. Press through the heels, lift the hips while keeping the pelvis level, then lower slowly.
exercises.easier: Use a smaller range.
exercises.harder: Add a band, use a staggered stance, or progress toward an offset bridge.

exercises.name: Sit to stand or shallow squat
exercises.how: Keep the feet grounded and knees tracking over the middle toes as you stand from a chair or perform a shallow squat.
exercises.easier: Use a higher chair or push lightly from the armrests.
exercises.harder: Lower the chair or hold light external weight.

exercises.name: Band sidestep
exercises.dose: 2 sets of 8 to 12 steps each direction
exercises.how: Place a loop band above the knees or at the ankles. Take controlled side steps while keeping the pelvis level and feet facing forward.
exercises.easier: Move the band above the knees or take smaller steps.
exercises.harder: Use a stronger band or place it closer to the ankles.

exercises.name: Standing band hip abduction
exercises.dose: 2 sets of 8 to 12 repetitions per side
exercises.how: Hold a counter. Move one leg slightly out to the side without leaning the trunk or turning the toes outward, then return slowly.
exercises.easier: Use no band and a smaller range.
exercises.harder: Increase band resistance or pause at the end range.

responseIntro: Some discomfort can be acceptable when it does not increase night pain or next-day symptoms. Night pain is a useful signal that total walking, standing, and exercise load may be too high.
green: Mild, controlled discomfort with stable sleep and no meaningful increase in next-day lateral hip pain.
yellow: Night pain or next-day walking pain increases. Reduce resistance, range, repetitions, long walks, hills, or sustained single-leg positions.
red: New inability to bear weight, rapidly progressive weakness, deep groin pain with major stiffness, fever, or neurologic symptoms. Stop and seek assessment.

progression.title: Stage 1: reduce compression
progression.text: Avoid lying directly on the painful side, crossing the legs, and hanging on one hip. Use a pillow between the knees when lying on the other side.

progression.title: Stage 2: build lower-limb strength
progression.text: Progress bridge, sit-to-stand, sidestep, and standing abduction by repetitions, band resistance, chair height, or external load.

progression.title: Stage 3: restore single-leg demand
progression.text: Add step-ups, longer walks, hills, and activity-specific single-leg tasks in small increments while night pain remains stable.

readyItems: Night pain is stable or improving.
Walking and stairs are becoming easier.
The pelvis stays level during the current exercises.
You can add resistance without a next-day lateral hip flare.
evaluation: Schedule a visit if groin pain or major stiffness suggests hip-joint disease, weakness or limping is progressing, neurologic symptoms are present, or an eight-week education and loading program is not improving function.

faqs.q: Should I stretch the outside of the hip?
faqs.a: Forceful hip-adduction stretching can compress the gluteal tendons and may aggravate symptoms. Early care usually emphasizes reducing compression and progressively strengthening instead.
Avoid lying directly on the painful side. When lying on the other side, place a pillow between the knees to reduce hip adduction and compression.

faqs.q: Is a corticosteroid injection the best first step?
faqs.a: Not usually. In the LEAP randomized trial, education plus exercise produced better global improvement than injection at eight weeks and maintained an advantage at longer follow-up.
Usually yes, but adjust total distance, hills, and consecutive long days if night pain or next-day symptoms increase. Keep general activity while the strength program builds capacity.

sources.label: Mellor et al.: Education Plus Exercise Versus Corticosteroid Injection for Gluteal Tendinopathy, 2018
LEAP Trial Protocol: Exercise and Load-Management Program
Graduated Exercise Program for Gluteal Tendinopathy, 2024

title: Lateral Ankle Sprain Home Exercise Program
metaDescription: A printable, evidence-based starter rehabilitation program for lateral ankle sprain with motion, strength, balance, brace guidance, and return-to-activity progression.
summary: Early protected weight bearing, range of motion, strength, and balance training support recovery after an uncomplicated lateral ankle sprain. This plan progresses from basic motion to controlled hopping.

authorityItems.text: Brace or tape when appropriate during early loading
Daily balance work to rebuild control
Function-based progression back to sport

proof.span: with strength three days per week
progress by ability, not only the calendar

fitIntro: This starter program is intended for adults who have already been evaluated and told an uncomplicated lateral ankle sprain is the likely diagnosis. Fracture, syndesmotic injury, Achilles rupture, and major midfoot injury should be reasonably excluded.
fit: Pain and swelling mainly at the outer ankle after a rolling injury, with improving ability to bear weight and no concerning fracture or high-ankle features.
assessFirst: Inability to take four steps, focal bony tenderness, pain above the ankle, major midfoot pain, recurrent instability, a suspected Achilles injury, or a severe injury that may need short immobilization.
redFlags: Inability to take four steps, focal bony tenderness, deformity, a numb or cold foot, pain above the ankle, worsening swelling or bruising, Achilles rupture signs, locking, or repeated giving way.
programHeading: Move early, then make the ankle trustworthy.
programIntro: Progressive weight bearing with an appropriate brace or tape is supported for many uncomplicated sprains. Severe sprains may need clinician-directed immobilization for a short period before this progression.
frequency: Motion and balance daily; strength 3 days per week
equipment: Chair or counter, resistance band, optional brace
checkpoint: Reassess by function over 2 to 6 weeks
goal: Normal walking, stairs, balance, running, and cutting

exercises.name: Ankle pumps or alphabet
exercises.how: Move the ankle gently up, down, in, and out, or trace the alphabet with the toes. Stay within a comfortable range without forcing the injured direction.
exercises.easier: Use smaller motions with the leg supported.
exercises.harder: Increase the range as swelling and pain settle.

exercises.name: Band-resisted eversion
exercises.how: Anchor a band to the inside. Move the forefoot outward against the band without rotating the entire leg, then return slowly.
exercises.easier: Use a lighter band or shorter range.
exercises.harder: Use a stronger band or slower return.

exercises.name: Heel raise
exercises.how: Rise onto both forefeet with support nearby, keep the ankle centered, then lower slowly.
exercises.easier: Use more hand support or a smaller range.
exercises.harder: Progress to one leg or add external load.

exercises.name: Single-leg balance
exercises.how: Stand on the injured leg near a counter. Keep the foot tripod grounded and the knee soft while maintaining a steady posture.
exercises.easier: Use fingertip support or a shorter interval.
exercises.harder: Add head turns, reaches, or a less stable surface.

exercises.name: Later-stage hop and stick
exercises.frequency: 2 to 3 days per week when ready
exercises.how: Make a small forward or side hop and land quietly on the injured leg. Hold the landing for two seconds before the next repetition.
exercises.easier: Use a step-and-hold without leaving the floor.
exercises.harder: Increase direction, distance, or sport-specific speed gradually.

responseIntro: Mild discomfort is acceptable, but not sharp pain, giving way, increased limping, or increased swelling later that day or the following morning.
green: Mild discomfort, improving walking, and swelling that is stable or decreasing by the next morning.
yellow: Limping, swelling, or pain is worse later that day or the next morning. Reduce walking volume, exercise range, resistance, or balance difficulty.
red: Sharp bony pain, repeated giving way, a numb or cold foot, deformity, inability to take four steps, or pain above the ankle. Stop and seek assessment.

progression.title: Stage 1: protect and restore motion
progression.text: Use an appropriate brace or tape, progressive weight bearing, and frequent comfortable motion. Avoid prolonged immobilization unless directed for a severe sprain.

progression.title: Stage 2: rebuild strength and balance
progression.text: Progress eversion, heel raises, and single-leg balance. Increase challenge only when walking and next-day swelling remain stable.

progression.title: Stage 3: restore speed and direction
progression.text: Add hopping, landing, running, cutting, and sport-specific tasks after normal walking, stairs, and basic single-leg control return.

readyItems: Walking and stairs are nearly normal without a growing limp.
Swelling does not increase the next morning.
Single-leg balance is controlled near support.
Small hops land quietly without pain or giving way.
evaluation: Schedule a visit if Ottawa ankle-rule findings suggest radiographs, pain is above the ankle, the foot is numb or cold, instability keeps recurring, or walking and swelling are not improving as expected.

faqs.q: Should I rest until all pain is gone?
faqs.a: Usually not. Early protected weight bearing and structured exercise are recommended for many uncomplicated sprains. The amount of protection depends on severity.
A brace or tape can support early weight bearing and reduce recurrence risk, especially during higher-risk activity. The choice should match injury severity, comfort, and sport demands.
Ice can be used briefly for comfort, but it should be an adjunct to exercise and progressive loading rather than the main treatment.

faqs.q: When can I return to sport?
faqs.a: Use function rather than the calendar alone. Walking, stairs, strength, balance, hopping, and sport-specific changes of direction should return without giving way or a next-day swelling flare.

sources.label: JOSPT Clinical Practice Guideline: Lateral Ankle Ligament Sprains, 2021
BMJ: Home-Based Proprioceptive Training to Prevent Ankle-Sprain Recurrence

title: Common Extensor Tendinopathy Home Exercise Program
conditionName: Lateral elbow tendinopathy and common extensor tendinopathy
metaDescription: A printable, evidence-based starter exercise program for common extensor and lateral elbow tendinopathy with progressive wrist loading, grip work, symptom rules, and return guidance.
kicker: Lateral elbow pain · Home exercise plan
summary: Common extensor tendinopathy usually improves through activity adjustment and progressive wrist-extensor loading. This plan moves from comfortable isometrics to slow strength, grip, and task-specific demand.

authorityItems.text: Progressive wrist-extensor loading
Grip and forearm capacity for daily tasks
A staged return to work, lifting, or sport

proof.span: to confirm an early functional trend

fitIntro: This starter program is intended for adults who have already been evaluated and told common extensor or lateral elbow tendinopathy is the likely diagnosis. It is not a self-diagnosis tool, a postoperative protocol, or a substitute for individualized care.
fit: Gradual pain and tenderness at or just below the outside elbow, aggravated by gripping, carrying, palm-down lifting, resisted wrist or finger extension, or stretching the wrist extensors.
assessFirst: A sudden injury, major swelling or weakness, inability to fully move the elbow, locking or instability, neck pain with radiating symptoms, numbness or tingling, or pain located farther down the forearm that may reflect radial nerve involvement.
redFlags: A sudden pop, bruising, deformity, rapidly increasing pain, marked weakness, a hot red swollen elbow, fever, new numbness or progressive weakness, persistent rest or night pain, or unexplained systemic symptoms.
programHeading: Load the wrist extensors, then restore the task.
programIntro: Guidelines support isometric, concentric, and eccentric resisted wrist-extensor exercise, but no single dose is proven best. Use the isometric as the main load when the elbow is highly irritable. As slow wrist extension becomes tolerable, use the isometric as a warm-up rather than stacking two hard sessions.
frequency: Isometric daily when irritable; strength about 3 days per week
equipment: Table, light dumbbell or water bottle, towel or putty, optional band
checkpoint: Look for a trend by 4 to 6 weeks
goal: Comfortable grip, carrying, lifting, work, and sport

exercises.name: Isometric wrist extension
exercises.dose: 5 holds of 30 seconds with about 60 seconds of rest
exercises.how: Support the forearm palm down with the wrist near neutral. Press the back of the hand into the opposite hand or a fixed surface without allowing movement. Use approximately 30 to 50 percent effort.
exercises.easier: Use 3 holds of 20 seconds at lighter effort.
exercises.harder: Build to 5 holds of 45 seconds, then increase resistance gradually.

exercises.name: Slow wrist extension
exercises.frequency: Every other day, usually 3 days per week
exercises.how: Support the forearm palm down with the hand over a table edge. Raise the wrist for 2 seconds and lower for 3 to 4 seconds. Keep the fingers relaxed.
exercises.easier: Use 2 sets of 10 with less weight or assist the lifting phase with the other hand.
exercises.harder: After two stable sessions, add the smallest available weight while keeping the slow tempo.

exercises.name: Forearm pronation and supination
exercises.dose: 2 sets of 12 repetitions in each direction
exercises.how: Keep the elbow at 90 degrees against the side. Slowly rotate a light hammer or dumbbell from palm up to palm down without moving the upper arm.
exercises.easier: Use no weight or hold closer to the weighted end.
exercises.harder: Hold farther from the weighted end, then add load.

exercises.name: Controlled grip
exercises.dose: 3 sets of 8 squeezes with a 5-second hold
exercises.how: Squeeze a rolled towel or soft putty at submaximal effort. Keep the wrist straight rather than bending it back during the squeeze.
exercises.easier: Use a lighter squeeze or a 3-second hold.
exercises.harder: Use firmer putty, then progress to short loaded carries with a neutral wrist.

exercises.name: Band row with a neutral wrist
exercises.how: Use a relaxed grip, keep the wrist straight, and draw the shoulder blade gently back without shrugging or clenching the band.
exercises.easier: Use a lighter band or 2 sets of 10.
exercises.harder: Use a stronger band or progress to a controlled single-arm row.

responseIntro: A small amount of familiar discomfort can be acceptable when it stays controlled, does not increase across repetitions, and returns to baseline by the next morning.
green: Pain stays between 0 and 3 out of 10, grip remains steady, and symptoms return to baseline by the following morning.
yellow: Pain reaches 4 to 5 out of 10, grip feels weaker afterward, or the elbow remains worse the next morning. Reduce weight, range, or repetitions by 25 to 50 percent and add recovery time.
red: Sharp, electric, or rapidly increasing pain, new numbness or weakness, swelling, loss of motion, or symptoms that worsen week to week. Stop and seek assessment.

progression.title: Stage 1: settle irritability
progression.text: Reduce repetitive hard gripping and palm-down lifting. Use a neutral grip or palm-up lifting when practical and begin comfortable isometrics.

progression.title: Stage 2: build forearm capacity
progression.text: Add slow wrist extension, rotation, and controlled grip every other day. Progress repetitions before adding weight.

progression.title: Stage 3: restore real demand
progression.text: Progress lever length, loaded carries, work tools, racquet handling, or lifting. Begin near half of usual volume and increase only with a stable next-day response.

readyItems: Elbow and wrist motion are full or nearly full.
Daily tasks stay at 0 to 2 out of 10 without a next-day flare.
Resisted wrist extension and gripping stay controlled.
Two to three loading sessions and two graded task sessions do not escalate symptoms.
evaluation: Schedule a visit if neurologic or mechanical symptoms are present, pain is worsening, grip strength is progressively declining, or a correctly dosed program does not produce a meaningful trend after about four to six weeks.

faqs.a: Usually not. Reduce the most provocative gripping and lifting dose while keeping tolerable movement and progressive loading.
Gentle stretching is optional and should not provoke a sustained flare. Stretching alone has not shown durable superiority over a loading-based plan.
A strap or wrist support may improve pain during an activity for some people, but long-term benefit is uncertain and it should not replace progressive loading.
Imaging is not routinely required for a typical presentation. It becomes more useful after trauma, with mechanical or neurologic findings, or when appropriate care has not worked.

sources.label: JOSPT Clinical Practice Guideline: Lateral Elbow Pain and Muscle Function Impairments, 2022
Cochrane Review: Manual Therapy and Exercise for Lateral Elbow Pain, 2024
Eccentric Exercise for Lateral Elbow Tendinopathy, Systematic Review and Meta-analysis, 2021
Unsupervised Isometric Exercise for Lateral Elbow Tendinopathy, Randomized Trial

title: Common Flexor Tendinopathy Home Exercise Program
conditionName: Medial elbow tendinopathy and common flexor tendinopathy
metaDescription: A printable starter exercise program for common flexor and medial elbow tendinopathy with progressive wrist-flexor and pronator loading, grip work, symptom rules, and safety guidance.
kicker: Medial elbow pain · Home exercise plan
h1: Build the flexor-pronator system back up.
summary: Common flexor tendinopathy can improve with activity adjustment and progressive loading of the wrist flexors and pronators. Because medial-elbow exercise research is limited, this plan uses a conservative dose and clear reasons to seek assessment.

authorityItems.text: Progressive wrist-flexor and pronator loading
Clear throwing and ulnar-nerve safety limits
A gradual return to grip and task demand

fitIntro: This starter program is intended for adults who have already been evaluated and told common flexor or medial elbow tendinopathy is the likely diagnosis. It is not appropriate for a suspected ulnar collateral ligament injury or ulnar neuropathy.
fit: Gradual focal pain and tenderness at or just below the inside elbow, aggravated by gripping, resisted wrist flexion, forearm pronation, or stretching into wrist extension.
assessFirst: A sudden throwing injury, loss of throwing velocity, valgus instability, numbness or tingling into the ring and small fingers, hand weakness, snapping at the medial elbow, major swelling, locking, or neck symptoms radiating into the arm.
redFlags: A sudden pop, bruising, deformity, marked weakness, a hot red swollen elbow, fever, progressive hand weakness, persistent ring-finger or small-finger numbness, acute throwing-related instability, unexplained rest or night pain, weight loss, or other systemic symptoms.
programHeading: Load the flexor-pronator system without missing the nerve or ligament.
programIntro: Direct exercise evidence for medial elbow tendinopathy is much thinner than it is for lateral elbow pain. Slow wrist-flexor loading has the best condition-specific support, while the exact dose, grip work, rotation, and shoulder exercise are pragmatic starting points.
frequency: Isometric daily when irritable; strength about 3 days per week
equipment: Table, light dumbbell or hammer, towel or putty, optional band
checkpoint: Look for a trend by 4 to 6 weeks
goal: Comfortable grip, pulling, lifting, work, racquet, or golf demand

exercises.name: Isometric wrist flexion
exercises.dose: 5 holds of 30 seconds with about 60 seconds of rest
exercises.how: Support the forearm palm up with the wrist near neutral. Press the palm into the opposite hand without allowing movement. Use approximately 30 to 50 percent effort.
exercises.easier: Use 3 holds of 20 seconds at lighter effort.
exercises.harder: Build to 5 holds of 45 seconds, then increase resistance gradually.

exercises.name: Slow wrist flexion
exercises.frequency: Every other day, usually 3 days per week
exercises.how: Support the forearm palm up with the hand over a table edge. Curl upward for 2 seconds and lower for 3 to 4 seconds.
exercises.easier: Use 2 sets of 10 with less weight or assist the upward phase with the other hand.
exercises.harder: After two stable sessions, add the smallest available weight while preserving the slow tempo.

exercises.name: Slow forearm pronation
exercises.how: Keep the elbow at 90 degrees. Start palm up and slowly turn palm down against a light hammer or band, then return with control.
exercises.easier: Use no weight or hold closer to the weighted end.
exercises.harder: Increase the lever length, then add load.

exercises.name: Controlled grip
exercises.dose: 3 sets of 8 squeezes with a 5-second hold
exercises.how: Squeeze a rolled towel or soft putty at submaximal effort. Keep the wrist straight and avoid maximal gripping.
exercises.easier: Use a lighter squeeze or a 3-second hold.
exercises.harder: Use firmer putty, then progress to short neutral-wrist carries.

exercises.name: Band row with a neutral wrist
exercises.how: Use a relaxed grip, keep the wrist straight, and draw the shoulder blade back without shrugging.
exercises.easier: Use a lighter band or 2 sets of 10.
exercises.harder: Use a stronger band or progress to a controlled single-arm row.

responseIntro: A small amount of familiar tendon discomfort can be acceptable when it stays controlled and returns to baseline by the next morning. Neurologic symptoms or throwing-related instability are not acceptable loading symptoms.
green: Pain stays between 0 and 3 out of 10, grip remains steady, and the elbow returns to baseline by the following morning.
yellow: Pain reaches 4 to 5 out of 10 or remains worse the next morning. Reduce weight, range, or repetitions by 25 to 50 percent and allow more recovery.
red: Ring-finger or small-finger numbness, hand weakness, snapping, sharp pain, major swelling, or throwing-related valgus pain or instability. Stop and seek assessment.

progression.title: Stage 1: settle irritability
progression.text: Reduce hard gripping, repeated wrist flexion and pronation, and provocative throwing. Begin comfortable isometric loading.

progression.title: Stage 2: build flexor-pronator capacity
progression.text: Add slow wrist flexion, pronation, and controlled grip every other day. Progress repetitions before adding load.

progression.title: Stage 3: restore the task
progression.text: Progress neutral-wrist carries, pulling, work tools, racquet or golf demand. Throwing athletes need ligament and nerve assessment plus a separate interval throwing plan.

readyItems: Elbow, forearm, and wrist motion are full and comfortable.
Daily activity stays at 0 to 2 out of 10 without a next-day flare.
Resisted wrist flexion, pronation, and gripping remain controlled.
Two to three loading sessions and two graded task sessions do not escalate symptoms.
evaluation: Schedule a visit for any nerve symptoms, snapping, throwing-related medial pain, instability, progressive weakness, or a flat response after about four to six weeks. Overhead athletes should be assessed before beginning an interval throwing program.

faqs.a: No. Common flexor tendinopathy also occurs with work, lifting, climbing, racquet sports, and other repeated gripping or forearm tasks.

faqs.q: Can I keep using the arm?
faqs.a: Usually yes, with the most provocative gripping, pronation, or throwing dose reduced. Mild stable discomfort can be acceptable when the next-day response remains controlled.
A brace can be tried for short-term activity tolerance, but it should not replace progressive loading and task modification.

faqs.q: Why do throwers need an assessment first?
faqs.a: Pain on the inside elbow during throwing may reflect the ulnar collateral ligament, ulnar nerve, or other structures rather than an isolated tendon problem.

sources.label: Eccentric Exercise for Medial Epicondylitis, Systematic Review, 2026
Tyler et al.: Eccentric Wrist-Flexor Exercise for Chronic Medial Epicondylosis
Current Concepts of Natural Course and in Management of Medial Epicondylitis: A Clinical Overview
Review: Medial Elbow Tendinopathy and Differential Diagnosis

title: Adhesive Capsulitis Home Exercise Program
metaDescription: A printable, evidence-based starter exercise program for adhesive capsulitis and frozen shoulder with irritability-matched mobility, progression, symptom rules, and safety guidance.
h1: Restore motion without fighting the shoulder.
summary: Frozen shoulder needs a different dose at different stages. Begin with brief, comfortable motion while pain and night symptoms dominate, then increase stretching time, active range, and strength as irritability falls.
authorityTitle: Calm pain. Restore motion. Rebuild function.

authorityItems.text: Use night pain to judge shoulder irritability
Progress from assisted motion to longer end-range time
Add active motion and strength as pain settles

proof.span: of short mobility work each day
to reassess pain, sleep, and motion

fitIntro: This starter program is intended for adults who have already been evaluated and told adhesive capsulitis or frozen shoulder is the likely diagnosis. It is not a postoperative protocol and should not be used to explain every painful or stiff shoulder.
fit: Gradual, often atraumatic shoulder pain followed by progressive loss of both active and passive motion. External rotation is often particularly limited, with night pain and difficulty dressing, grooming, or reaching.
assessFirst: Sudden weakness after trauma, passive motion that is relatively preserved, prior shoulder surgery, substantial shoulder arthritis, cervical or neurologic symptoms, recurrent instability, or a pattern that does not include progressive loss of passive motion.
redFlags: Recent trauma with deformity or inability to raise the arm, rapidly progressive weakness, dense numbness, fever, a hot red swollen shoulder, chest pain, shortness of breath, unexplained weight loss, or known cancer with new persistent pain.
programHeading: Match the mobility dose to irritability.
programIntro: Use only the first two exercises when rest pain and night pain are high. Aggressive stretching beyond the pain threshold can be counterproductive during a highly irritable phase. Add longer holds, active motion, and strength as pain settles and stiffness becomes the dominant limitation.
frequency: Short mobility sessions 1 to 2 times daily; strength 2 to 3 days per week when tolerated
equipment: Table, wall, cane or broom handle, optional light band
checkpoint: Reassess pain, sleep, function, and motion after 4 to 6 weeks
goal: Easier sleep, dressing, grooming, reaching, and lifting

exercises.name: Supported table slide
exercises.how: Sit facing a table with the hand supported on a towel. Slide the hand forward as the trunk inclines, then return before the shoulder guards or pain escalates.
exercises.easier: Use a shorter range and pause only 1 to 5 seconds in a comfortable position.
exercises.harder: Gradually hold 10 to 30 seconds near a tolerable end range before adding force.

exercises.name: Cane-assisted external rotation
exercises.how: Lie on the back or sit supported with both elbows near the sides. Use the unaffected arm and cane to rotate the involved forearm outward without letting the elbow drift away.
exercises.easier: Use a smaller range with the arm fully supported and only a brief pause.
exercises.harder: Increase the hold toward 10 to 30 seconds, then increase range without forcing the arm.

exercises.name: Supine assisted elevation
exercises.how: Lie on the back, clasp the hands or hold a cane, and let the unaffected arm assist the involved arm overhead through a comfortable range.
exercises.easier: Stop well before pain or support the arm on a pillow.
exercises.harder: Increase elevation gradually, then use less help from the other arm.

exercises.name: Active wall slide
exercises.frequency: Daily when rest pain is settling
exercises.how: Place the hand or forearm on a wall and slide upward while keeping the neck relaxed. Lower slowly without shrugging or twisting the trunk.
exercises.easier: Use the other hand to assist or shorten the range.
exercises.harder: Reach higher or add a brief comfortable end-range pause.

exercises.name: Light band row
exercises.how: Use a comfortable range and light resistance. Draw the shoulder blades gently back while keeping the neck relaxed and avoiding trunk compensation.
exercises.easier: Use a 5 to 10 second isometric hold or a lighter band.
exercises.harder: Add repetitions, then resistance, only when the next-day response remains stable.

responseIntro: Gentle pulling or mild discomfort can be acceptable when it settles promptly and does not meaningfully increase rest pain, night pain, or next-morning stiffness.
green: The mobility dose settles promptly, sleep is not worse, and the shoulder is no stiffer the next morning.
yellow: Pain stays elevated later that day, sleep worsens, or motion is worse the next morning. Reduce range, hold time, repetitions, or session frequency.
red: Sharp pain, sudden weakness, new numbness, traumatic loss of function, fever, or a hot red shoulder. Stop and seek assessment.

progression.title: Stage 1: high irritability
progression.text: When rest pain and night pain dominate, use brief comfortable table slides and assisted external rotation. Avoid prolonged or forceful capsular stretching.

progression.title: Stage 2: moderate irritability
progression.text: As pain becomes more movement-dependent, increase hold duration gradually and add assisted elevation and active wall slides.

progression.title: Stage 3: stiffness-dominant or thawing
progression.text: Build total end-range time, active motion, light resistance, and task-specific reaching while the next-day response remains stable.

progression.title: Stage 4: restore function
progression.text: Progress lifting, overhead work, dressing, grooming, and sport demands rather than measuring success by motion alone.

readyItems: Night pain is stable or improving.
The current mobility dose settles without a prolonged flare.
Range or daily function is trending better.
One variable can increase without worse next-day stiffness.
evaluation: Schedule a review if the diagnosis is uncertain, symptoms escalate, or pain, sleep, function, and motion are not trending better after four to six weeks. When early pain prevents meaningful exercise, discuss whether a glenohumeral corticosteroid injection is appropriate for your health history.

faqs.q: Should I force the shoulder to regain motion?
faqs.a: No. Stretch intensity should match irritability. Brief pain-limited motion is safer early, while longer end-range stretching is more appropriate once pain settles.

faqs.q: How long does frozen shoulder last?
faqs.a: Recovery commonly takes many months. Some people retain mild pain or motion loss at twelve to eighteen months even when disability is much better.
Usually not for a typical clinical pattern. Imaging is an adjunct when the presentation is atypical, trauma occurred, or another condition must be excluded.
A glenohumeral corticosteroid injection can improve short-term pain and function, particularly earlier in the course, but it does not replace a staged mobility plan.

sources.label: Clinical Practice Guidelines for Diagnosis and Non-Surgical Treatment of Primary Frozen Shoulder, 2025
JOSPT Clinical Practice Guideline: Adhesive Capsulitis
Manual Therapy and Exercise for Adhesive Capsulitis, Systematic Review and Meta-analysis, 2023
UK FROST Randomized Trial for Primary Frozen Shoulder

title: Patellar Tendinopathy Exercise Program for Athletes
metaDescription: A printable, evidence-based patellar tendinopathy program for athletes with progressive strength, jumping, sprinting, symptom monitoring, and return-to-sport guidance.
h1: Build the tendon from strength to speed.
summary: Slow strength is necessary, but it is not the finish line. Athletes must progress through strength, energy-storage loading, jumping, sprinting, and repeated sport exposure while monitoring the next-day tendon response.

authorityItems.text: Progressive quadriceps and tendon strength
Planned landing and jump contacts
A function-based return to full training

proof.span: from isometric loading to sport exposure
for a meaningful progressive strength trial

fitIntro: This starter program is intended for athletes who have already been evaluated and told patellar tendinopathy is the likely diagnosis. It is not intended for an acute tendon tear, a swollen or locked knee, or skeletally immature athletes with growth-center pain.
fit: Localized pain at the lower pole of the patella or along the patellar tendon, reproduced by jumping, landing, acceleration, deceleration, deep squatting, or resisted knee extension.
assessFirst: Diffuse pain around or behind the kneecap, substantial joint swelling, locking, instability, acute trauma, pain at the tibial tubercle or growth center, fat-pad pain, or symptoms suggesting inflammatory disease.
redFlags: A sudden pop with inability to perform a straight-leg raise, a palpable tendon defect, rapid traumatic swelling, inability to bear weight, a locked knee, fever, a hot red joint, marked calf swelling, or shortness of breath.
programHeading: Earn jumping through progressive tendon load.
programIntro: Do not begin every exercise at full volume. Start with the isometric plus two slow strength movements. Add low-level jumping only after slow loading is controlled, baseline symptoms are stable, and there is at least forty-eight hours between early high tendon-load sessions.
frequency: Strength 3 days per week; jumping initially 2 days per week
equipment: Strong strap or wall, chair, backpack or weights, optional band or gym equipment
checkpoint: Adjust at 4 to 6 weeks; judge the strength trial near 12 weeks
goal: Jumping, landing, sprinting, deceleration, and full training capacity

exercises.name: Spanish squat or wall-sit isometric
exercises.dose: 5 holds of 30 to 45 seconds with 1 to 2 minutes of rest
exercises.frequency: Daily when useful or before training
exercises.how: Sit back against a strong strap behind the knees or use a wall sit. Keep the trunk tall and hold at a tolerable knee angle.
exercises.easier: Use a shorter hold, shallower knee angle, or bilateral wall sit.
exercises.harder: Increase hold time, knee angle, or resistance without worsening the next-morning tendon test.

exercises.name: Slow squat or leg press
exercises.dose: Start with 3 sets of 12 to 15 repetitions
exercises.how: Lower for about 3 seconds and rise for about 3 seconds through a tolerable range. Keep the knee tracking over the middle toes.
exercises.easier: Use a bilateral box squat, shallower range, or lighter load.
exercises.harder: Increase load while moving gradually toward 4 sets of 6 to 8 repetitions.

exercises.name: Slow split squat
exercises.dose: 3 sets of 6 to 12 repetitions per side
exercises.how: Keep the front foot grounded and lower slowly with the knee tracking over the middle toes. Use support if needed for balance.
exercises.easier: Use a shorter depth, hand support, or more assistance from the back leg.
exercises.harder: Add range, backpack or dumbbell load, then emphasize the front leg.

exercises.name: Resisted knee extension
exercises.how: Use a band, ankle weight, or machine. Straighten and lower the knee slowly without snapping into extension.
exercises.easier: Use lighter resistance or a shorter tolerable arc.
exercises.harder: Increase resistance and work gradually toward a heavier 6 to 8 repetition range.

exercises.name: Snap-down to low pogo series
exercises.dose: Begin with 2 to 3 sets of 10 controlled contacts
exercises.frequency: 2 days per week with at least 48 hours between sessions
exercises.how: Start with a quiet landing and hold. Progress to small, quick bilateral pogo jumps while maintaining alignment and a spring-like ankle and knee strategy.
exercises.easier: Practice a landing without a rebound jump.
exercises.harder: Add rebound speed, then height, then single-leg or multidirectional contacts. Change only one variable per session.

exercises.name: Sport-specific jump and acceleration exposure
exercises.dose: Begin with 3 to 5 sets of 3 to 5 high-quality efforts
exercises.frequency: 1 to 2 days per week after low-level jumping is tolerated
exercises.how: Select a jump, landing, sprint, or deceleration pattern required by the sport. Track the number, intensity, surface, and next-morning response.
exercises.easier: Reduce speed, height, approach distance, or total efforts.
exercises.harder: Increase either intensity or volume, one variable at a time, then build repeated efforts and practice density.

responseIntro: A repeatable load test, such as a decline squat, can help track the tendon. Mild pain can be acceptable when movement stays normal and the tendon returns to its usual baseline by the next morning.
green: Pain stays near 3 out of 10 or less, movement remains normal, and the tendon is back to its usual baseline the next morning.
yellow: Pain rises across sets, landing strategy changes, or the standard load test is meaningfully worse the next morning. Reduce weight, range, jump intensity, or contact count.
red: A sudden sharp pain or pop, new knee-extension weakness, rapid swelling, or inability to perform a straight-leg raise. Stop and seek urgent assessment.

progression.title: Stage 1: control the load spike
progression.text: Reduce the most provocative jump, sprint, or competition volume while maintaining tolerable conditioning and skill work.

progression.title: Stage 2: build slow strength
progression.text: Progress from isometrics to slow isotonic loading. Over time the strength exercises should become meaningfully heavy while the next-day response remains stable.

progression.title: Stage 3: restore energy storage
progression.text: Add landings, pogos, hops, accelerations, and decelerations with planned contact counts and recovery days.

progression.title: Stage 4: return to sport and performance
progression.text: Progress from individual drills to partial training, full training, competition, and then the workload needed for performance.

readyItems: The repeatable tendon load test stays near 2 to 3 out of 10 or less.
High-intensity loading returns to baseline by the next morning.
The knee has full motion without an effusion or altered gait.

Quadriceps strength and hop or jump capacity are approaching the other side and relevant sport benchmarks, often using 90 percent as one part of the decision, with acceptable movement quality. Symmetry alone can be misleading when both sides are deconditioned.
Repeated full training sessions are tolerated without a protective strategy.
evaluation: No single validated return-to-sport test exists for patellar tendinopathy. Review loading and technique at four to six weeks. Reassess the diagnosis and program when there is no trend after eight to twelve weeks of consistent progressive loading, or when jump volume, strength deficits, or return-to-sport demands require individualized testing.

faqs.a: Usually not. Relative load reduction is preferred when training does not worsen the next-day response. Remove large jump-volume spikes and count strength, jumping, practice, and competition as tendon load.
No. They may provide temporary pain relief for some athletes, but progressive isotonic strength and later energy-storage loading are essential.
No. A decline squat is useful as a load test and can be an exercise, but progressive loading can also use squats, split squats, leg press, and knee extension.
Begin after slow strength is controlled and baseline symptoms are stable. Start with low contacts, preserve recovery days, and check the tendon the next morning.
No. Diagnosis and progression are primarily clinical because tendon changes can be present in athletes without pain and structural change does not consistently track recovery.

sources.label: Dutch Multidisciplinary Guideline for Patellofemoral Pain and Patellar Tendinopathy, 2024
Breda et al.: Progressive Tendon-Loading Exercise Versus Eccentric Exercise, Randomized Trial
Clinical Management of Patellar Tendinopathy
Malliaras et al.: Patellar Tendinopathy Diagnosis, Load Management, and Advice for Challenging Cases
Patellar Tendon Load Progression During Rehabilitation Exercises, 2024
